title: Midterm Review: Traversal, Search, Sort & 2D Arrays
description: A consolidated review of every array skill from the midterm period, covering traversal, search, sort, and 2D arrays before the exam.
congratsText: Great work! You've reviewed every array skill from the midterm — you're ready for the exam.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Recall the core pattern behind array declaration, traversal, and aggregation
  • Recall the core pattern behind linear search and its variations
  • Recall the core pattern behind bubble sort and its variations
  • Recall the core pattern behind 2D array traversal and matrix operations

sections.title: Review: Declaration, Indexing & Traversal

quiz.question: For int arr[6];, what are the valid index values?
options: 0 through 5
1 through 6
0 through 6
1 through 5

sections.lesson:
Review: Declaration, Indexing & Traversal
Arrays are zero-indexed, fixed-size blocks of same-type memory. A for loop with i < size safely traverses every element without going out of bounds.
int arr[6]; for (int i = 0; i < 6; i++) { // valid indexes: 0 through 5 }

sections.title: Review: The Accumulator Pattern

quiz.question: What is the result of this accumulator loop on arr = {4, 8, 2, 6}?

sections.lesson:
Review: The Accumulator Pattern
Sum, average, and conditional count all share the same shape: initialize a variable before the loop, update it on every pass. The same single traversal can update several accumulators at once.

sections.title: Review: Linear Search

quiz.question: Why does linear search return -1 instead of 0 to signal 'not found'?
options: 0 is a valid index (the first element), so it can't also mean 'not found'
-1 makes the function run faster than returning 0 would
C does not allow functions to return the value 0
It has no real reason — either value would work equally well

sections.lesson:
Review: Linear Search
Linear search compares each element to a target in order, stopping and returning the index on a match, or returning -1 if the loop finishes without one.
int search(int arr[], int size, int target) { for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] == target) return i; } return -1; }

sections.title: Review: Search Variations

quiz.question: What is the key code change between 'find first match' and 'find all matches'?
options: Removing the early return, so the loop keeps scanning after a match
Changing the comparison operator from == to !=
Adding a second nested loop over the same array
Sorting the array before the search loop begins

sections.lesson:
Review: Search Variations
First match returns immediately on the first hit. Find-all removes that early return so the loop keeps going. Min/max compares elements to each other instead of to a fixed target, usually starting the loop at index 1 after seeding with arr[0].

sections.title: Review: Bubble Sort

quiz.question: After ONE full pass of ascending bubble sort on {6, 2, 9, 1}, what does the array look like?

sections.lesson:
Review: Bubble Sort
Bubble sort compares and swaps neighboring pairs across repeated passes. Each pass guarantees the largest unsorted value reaches its correct position at the end — which is why multiple passes are required to fully sort.
for (int i = 0; i < size - 1; i++) { for (int j = 0; j < size - i - 1; j++) { if (arr[j] > arr[j + 1]) { int temp = arr[j]; arr[j] = arr[j + 1]; arr[j + 1] = temp; } } }

sections.title: Review: Sort Variations

quiz.question: What single change turns ascending bubble sort into descending bubble sort?
options: Flipping the comparison operator from > to <
Reversing the array before sorting starts
Changing the outer loop to count downward
Adding an extra parameter for direction to every function

sections.lesson:
Review: Sort Variations
Descending order only requires flipping one comparison symbol. Searching a sorted array can stop early once a value exceeds the target, since nothing later in the sorted array could possibly match.

sections.title: Review: 2D Arrays & Traversal

quiz.question: What is the correct loop structure to visit every cell of a 2D array exactly once?
options: An outer loop over rows, and an inner loop over columns
A single loop counting from 0 to rows + cols
An outer loop over columns, and an inner loop over rows only for even columns
Two completely independent, non-nested loops

sections.lesson:
Review: 2D Arrays & Traversal
A 2D array is addressed with grid[row][col] and stored in row-major order in memory. Nested loops — outer for rows, inner for columns — visit every cell exactly once.

sections.title: Review: Matrix Operations

quiz.question: Which cells make up a square matrix's main diagonal?
options: Cells where the row index equals the column index
Every cell in the first row only
Cells where the row index is one greater than the column index
Every cell in the last column only

sections.lesson:
Review: Matrix Operations
Matrix addition combines matching cells (result[i][j] = a[i][j] + b[i][j]) and requires equal dimensions. Grid search nests the linear-search comparison across rows and columns. Diagonal sum adds cells where the row and column index match.

sections.title: Recap: The Exam Scope

quiz.question: Which four topic areas does the midterm exam cover, based on this review?
options: Array declaration/traversal, search (and variations), sort (and variations), and 2D arrays/matrices
Linked lists, stacks, queues, and trees
Only bubble sort and nothing else from the midterm period
Only topics from Weeks 1 through 3 on C foundations

sections.lesson:
Recap: The Exam Scope
The midterm exam draws from four connected areas: array declaration/traversal and aggregation, linear search and its variations, bubble sort and its variations, and 2D array/matrix operations. Every one of these builds on the same core habits — zero-based indexing, loop traversal, and the accumulator pattern — applied in different combinations.
